title: Where the Boss Got Started: CEOs Reflect on First Jobs
content:
Everyone remembers their first meaningful job, whether it was scooping ice cream in high school or spending the first stretch in a real office after college. For many successful business leaders, the hard-won lessons learned during these early days continue to underpin their success today. For example, David Solomon, CEO of Goldman Sachs, started at Baskin-Robbins when he was fifteen. He learned the value of focus and paying attention, realizing that a long line meant he needed to get flavors and toppings exactly right. Similarly, Billy Hult of Tradeweb Markets worked as a betting clerk in the South Bronx, which taught him how to process information and think quickly.

Other leaders attribute their resilience to unconventional first jobs. David Karandish, founder of Capacity, experienced an intense crash course in competition and endurance while working on The Apprentice: Martha Stewart. Watching a billionaire operate in her element drove home how little patience there is for ideas that do not translate into action. Meanwhile, Robin Vince, CEO of BNY, began as an IBM intern teaching executives how to use new Microsoft tools. Saying yes to things that intimidated him became a guiding principle, one he most recently applied to learning about artificial intelligence. 

Even physically demanding or seemingly routine jobs have offered invaluable lessons. Mary Cannon, co-founder of CityPickle, worked as a packer for a moving company where she learned the importance of shared responsibility and teamwork. Ross Andrew Macdonald of Uber spent hours scrubbing golf carts by hand, creating little games in his head that soon became incentives. He discovered that even the most routine work can feel meaningful if you set goals and make it your own. Ultimately, these early experiences show that foundational skills can be built anywhere.
